events.text: The patient is a 71-year-old male named Gerald Hutchinson. He is a retired civil engineer of Northern European descent, with a significant family history of prostate cancer (father diagnosed at age 65, paternal uncle deceased from prostate cancer). He is a former smoker (20 pack-years, quit at age 55), with a history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes managed with oral agents. He lives with his wife and is functionally independent with an ECOG performance status of 1.

At age 67 years, the patient had a diagnosis of ADENOCARCINOMA OF THE PROSTATE (ICD-O-3 histology code 8140/3), site: PROSTATE (C61.9). Clinical TNM stage: cT3bN1M0, AJCC Stage IVA. Gleason score: 4+5=9 (Grade Group 5). PSA at diagnosis: 87.4 ng/mL. Tumor involved bilateral lobes with seminal vesicle invasion and right obturator lymph node involvement (1.8 cm on MRI). Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY via transrectal ultrasound-guided biopsy (12 cores, 10/12 positive). Tumor Size: bulky bilateral prostate involvement. Percent cores positive: 83%. Perineural invasion: present. Lymphovascular invasion: present. Summary Stage: Regional. Laterality: bilateral. Clinical stage summary: high-risk locally advanced prostate cancer with regional lymph node metastases.

At age 67 years, the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY. Specimen: transrectal ultrasound-guided prostate needle biopsy, 12 cores. Diagnosis: prostatic adenocarcinoma, Gleason score 4+5=9 (Grade Group 5). 10 of 12 cores involved. Maximum core involvement: 95% (left base). Perineural invasion: present. No lymphovascular invasion identified on biopsy specimen. Immunohistochemistry: PSA positive, PSMA positive, NKX3.1 positive, p63 negative (confirming acinar adenocarcinoma, not urothelial). No neuroendocrine differentiation (synaptophysin and chromogranin negative).

At age 67 years, the patient had a multiparametric MRI of the prostate and pelvis (mpMRI), which showed cancer. Findings: bulky bilateral prostate tumor with extracapsular extension at the right apex and bilateral mid-gland. Seminal vesicle invasion bilateral, greater on the right. A 1.8 cm right obturator lymph node and two smaller right iliac chain lymph nodes (0.9 cm and 0.7 cm) with morphologic features suspicious for metastatic involvement. No evidence of bone metastases or distant organ involvement on pelvic MRI sequences. PI-RADS score: 5. Dominant lesion size: 3.4 cm.

At age 67 years, the patient had a whole-body Tc-99m bone scan and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis for staging, which showed no definitive distant metastases. CT findings: bilateral prostate mass with pelvic lymphadenopathy as described on MRI. No pulmonary nodules. No hepatic lesions. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy above the aortic bifurcation. Bone scan: no scintigraphic evidence of osseous metastatic disease. Impression: cT3bN1M0, Stage IVA, consistent with regionally advanced disease without distant metastases.

At age 67 years, the patient was initiated on androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) with leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months, combined with abiraterone acetate 1000 mg orally once daily plus prednisone 5 mg orally twice daily, as first-line systemic therapy for high-risk locally advanced prostate cancer (cT3bN1M0, Gleason 9, PSA 87.4). Treatment intent: curative in combination with radiation therapy. Castrate testosterone level achieved within 4 weeks (testosterone 14 ng/dL). PSA nadir: 0.4 ng/mL at 6 months.

At age 67 years (approximately 3 months after initiating ADT and abiraterone), the patient received definitive external beam radiation therapy (EBRT) to the prostate and pelvic lymph nodes. Technique: intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) with image-guided radiation therapy (IGRT). Dose: prostate/seminal vesicles 78 Gy in 39 fractions; pelvic elective nodal volume 54 Gy in 39 fractions. Duration: approximately 8 weeks. Concurrent with ADT. Tolerated treatment with grade 1-2 urinary frequency and urgency, and grade 1 rectal toxicity. No grade 3 or higher acute toxicities.

At age 68 years (12 months after starting ADT/abiraterone), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. PSA 0.2 ng/mL (nadir). Testosterone: castrate at 11 ng/dL. Patient reporting fatigue (grade 1), mild hot flashes, and occasional nocturia (residual from radiation). No bone pain. ECOG PS 1. Physical exam: no palpable lymphadenopathy. Digital rectal exam: prostate without palpable nodularity. Impression: good biochemical response to ADT plus abiraterone and definitive radiation. No evidence of cancer activity. Plan: continue ADT plus abiraterone, surveillance imaging in 12 months.

At age 69 years (24 months after initial systemic therapy), the patient had a surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and bone scan, which showed no evidence of metastatic disease. Bone scan: no new osseous lesions. CT: no new lymphadenopathy. Post-radiation changes in the prostate bed. No hepatic, pulmonary, or adrenal lesions. Impression: no evidence of distant metastatic disease. PSA at this time point: 0.3 ng/mL (slight rise from nadir but below threshold for biochemical recurrence). Continued surveillance recommended.

At age 70 years (36 months after starting systemic therapy), the patient had an oncologist office assessment noting PSA rising trend: PSA values over prior 6 months were 0.3 ng/mL, 0.6 ng/mL, 1.1 ng/mL, and now 2.3 ng/mL. Testosterone confirmed castrate: 9 ng/dL. PSA doubling time calculated at approximately 3.5 months, consistent with rapid biochemical progression. Patient otherwise asymptomatic. No bone pain. ECOG PS 1. Impression: castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC) per PCWG3 criteria -- PSA progression with confirmed castrate testosterone while on continuous ADT plus abiraterone. Plan: discontinue abiraterone, initiate germline genetic testing given family history and high-risk disease features, and consider next line of therapy.

At age 70 years, the patient had a pathology result of type GERMLINE GENETIC TESTING. Specimen: peripheral blood. Laboratory: Myriad myRisk Hereditary Cancer Panel. Result: PATHOGENIC VARIANT DETECTED. BRCA2 c.5946delT (p.Ser1982Argfs*22) -- frameshift mutation, pathogenic. This is a well-characterized pathogenic BRCA2 variant associated with hereditary breast and ovarian cancer syndrome and hereditary prostate cancer. BRCA1: no pathogenic variant detected. CDK12: N/A (germline test). ATM: no pathogenic variant detected. PALB2: no pathogenic variant detected. MSH2, MLH1, MSH6, PMS2: no pathogenic variants detected. Interpretation: confirmed germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant, consistent with hereditary prostate cancer susceptibility. Genetic counseling referral placed. Cascade testing recommended for first-degree relatives.

At age 70 years, the patient had next generation sequencing performed for castration-resistant prostate cancer based on a specimen obtained from a prostate biopsy (primary site re-biopsy at time of CRPC). Panel: Foundation One CDx. Findings: BRCA2 p.Ser1982Argfs*22 mutation (pathogenic, loss of heterozygosity confirmed -- biallelic loss), AR amplification (copy number: 8x), TP53 p.R175H missense mutation (pathogenic), RB1 loss (deep deletion), CDK12 no mutation detected, PTEN loss (homozygous deletion), MSI status: microsatellite stable (MSS), TMB: 4 mutations/Mb (low), CDK4 gain, MYC amplification (4x), PIK3CA no mutation. Genomic loss of heterozygosity (gLOH): 18% (elevated, consistent with HRD). No SPOP mutation detected. No AR point mutation (T878A, F877L) detected. No neuroendocrine differentiation markers. Actionable alterations: BRCA2 biallelic loss (FDA-approved: olaparib, rucaparib). AR amplification noted -- may predict resistance to second AR-targeted agents.

At age 70 years, at time of CRPC declaration, the patient had a restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and whole-body bone scan, which showed no metastatic disease but was notable for PSA progression. Bone scan: no definitive osseous metastatic lesions. CT: no new lymphadenopathy, no visceral metastases. Post-treatment changes in prostate bed. Impression: biochemically castration-resistant, non-metastatic by conventional imaging (nmCRPC). Note: Prostate-specific membrane antigen (PSMA) PET/CT also obtained at this time point.

At age 70 years, the patient had a PSMA PET/CT (68Ga-PSMA-11), which showed cancer with limited metastatic extent. Findings: increased PSMA avidity at the right iliac chain (SUVmax 4.2, 1.1 cm node) and a single focus of mildly increased uptake at the right posterior iliac crest (SUVmax 3.8), suspicious for early osseous metastasis not seen on bone scan. No visceral metastases. No evidence of neuroendocrine dedifferentiation. Impression: low-volume metastatic CRPC (mCRPC) by PSMA PET/CT, M1 by PET criteria. Right posterior iliac crest lesion represents an early bone metastasis.

At age 70 years, following confirmed CRPC with germline BRCA2 loss and PSMA PET-detected bone/nodal metastases, the patient was initiated on enzalutamide 160 mg orally once daily. ADT (leuprolide acetate) continued for castrate testosterone maintenance. Abiraterone was discontinued. Rationale: enzalutamide selected as next-generation AR-signaling inhibitor (ARSI) for mCRPC despite prior abiraterone use, given NGS showing AR amplification without AR point mutations, and given that olaparib might be more impactful after further disease evolution. Baseline PSA at enzalutamide initiation: 2.3 ng/mL.

At age 70 years (3 months after enzalutamide initiation), the patient had an oncologist office assessment showing early PSA response. PSA: 0.9 ng/mL (61% decline from baseline). Testosterone: castrate at 10 ng/dL. Patient reporting grade 1 fatigue and mild cognitive fogginess, which he attributes to the enzalutamide. No falls. No seizures. ECOG PS 1. No new bone pain. Impression: PSA response to enzalutamide, consistent with partial biochemical response. Plan: continue enzalutamide, repeat imaging at 6 months.

At age 70-71 years (6 months after enzalutamide initiation), the patient had a restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and bone scan, which showed stable disease. Bone scan: stable appearance of the right posterior iliac crest lesion, no new lesions. CT: no visceral metastases, no new lymphadenopathy. PSA at 6-month mark: 0.7 ng/mL (nadir on enzalutamide). Impression: stable disease on enzalutamide. No radiographic progression. Continue current therapy.

At age 71 years (9 months after enzalutamide initiation), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. PSA rising again: 0.7 ng/mL (nadir) to 1.4 ng/mL to now 2.8 ng/mL over 3 months. PSA doubling time: approximately 6 weeks, indicating rapid biochemical progression on enzalutamide. Patient reporting new onset of mid-back pain (grade 1-2), worsening fatigue, and decreased appetite. ECOG PS 1-2. No neurological deficits. Impression: PSA and symptomatic progression on enzalutamide. Plan: urgent restaging imaging.

At age 71 years (9 months after enzalutamide initiation), the patient had a restaging whole-body bone scan and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, which showed progression with new bone metastases. Bone scan: new foci of increased uptake at T10 vertebral body, right posterior 6th rib, and left iliac wing (3 new lesions beyond the known right iliac crest lesion). CT: stable lymph node burden in right iliac chain, no visceral metastases. No spinal cord compression on scout imaging. Impression: radiographic progression on enzalutamide with new multifocal bone metastases. Meets PCWG3 criteria for progressive disease (2 or more new bone lesions). MRI spine recommended to exclude cord compromise.

At age 71 years, the patient had an MRI of the thoracic and lumbar spine (whole spine protocol), which showed bone metastases without cord compression. Findings: T10 vertebral body metastasis with marrow replacement, no epidural extension, no spinal cord compression. L3 vertebral body with early marrow signal change consistent with early metastatic involvement. No pathological fracture. Paravertebral soft tissue extension at T10 is minimal. Impression: osseous metastatic disease involving T10 and L3 without neurological compromise. Radiation oncology consultation placed for T10 lesion given proximity to cord.

At age 71 years, following radiographic progression on enzalutamide with new bone metastases, enzalutamide was discontinued and the patient was initiated on olaparib 300 mg orally twice daily (FDA-approved PARP inhibitor for mCRPC with BRCA2 alteration, based on PROfound trial data). ADT (leuprolide) continued. Denosumab 120 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks also initiated for bone metastasis prevention (with calcium and vitamin D supplementation). Baseline labs: hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, platelets 198,000/mcL, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (eGFR 68 mL/min). Baseline PSA at olaparib initiation: 2.8 ng/mL.

At age 71 years, the patient received palliative external beam radiation therapy to the T10 vertebral body for bone pain and prophylactic stabilization. Technique: 3D conformal radiation therapy (3D-CRT). Dose: 30 Gy in 10 fractions. Concurrent with olaparib initiation (slight delay of olaparib by 2 weeks during radiation to limit hematologic toxicity overlap). Tolerance: well tolerated, grade 1 esophageal irritation (lesion near cord at T10), no significant myelosuppression during treatment. Pain response: improvement in mid-back pain within 2 weeks of completing radiation.

At age 71 years (2 months after olaparib initiation), the patient had an oncologist office assessment showing PSA response. PSA: 1.1 ng/mL (61% decline from 2.8 ng/mL at baseline). Hemoglobin: 10.4 g/dL (declined from 11.8 g/dL, grade 1-2 anemia). Patient reporting improved back pain (responding to radiation), but increased fatigue and mild nausea (grade 1). ECOG PS 1. No dyspnea. No lightheadedness. Impression: favorable PSA response to olaparib consistent with BRCA2-associated PARP inhibitor sensitivity. Emerging anemia consistent with olaparib class effect. Plan: monitor CBC closely, nutritional support, antiemetics as needed.

At age 71 years (3 months after olaparib initiation), the patient experienced a grade 3 anemia (hemoglobin nadir 7.1 g/dL) attributed to olaparib therapy. Patient presented with symptomatic dyspnea on exertion, fatigue, and lightheadedness. Reticulocyte count low (reticulocyte index 0.8), consistent with hypoproliferative anemia. Iron studies: iron saturation 22%, ferritin 98 ng/mL (mildly elevated suggesting anemia of chronic disease/PARP inhibition rather than iron deficiency alone). B12 and folate normal. Peripheral smear: normocytic, normochromic, no dysplastic changes. Bone marrow biopsy not performed (clinical picture consistent with olaparib-related myelosuppression). Action: olaparib dose reduced to 200 mg twice daily per label guidance. Red blood cell transfusion administered (2 units pRBC). Hemoglobin post-transfusion: 9.2 g/dL. Epoetin alfa not initiated (oncologist discretion, concern for disease progression).

At age 71 years (4 months after olaparib initiation), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. PSA: 0.8 ng/mL (continued decline). Hemoglobin: 8.9 g/dL (stable but below normal, grade 2 anemia). Patient tolerating olaparib 200 mg BID. Nausea improved with ondansetron as needed. Fatigue persistent but manageable (grade 1-2). No new bone pain. ECOG PS 1. Transfusion required (1 unit pRBC) to maintain hemoglobin above 8.5 g/dL. Impression: PSA response ongoing on reduced-dose olaparib with transfusion-dependent anemia. Plan: continue olaparib at 200 mg BID, repeat CBC in 2 weeks, imaging at 6 months.

At age 71-72 years (6 months after olaparib initiation), the patient had a restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and bone scan, which showed mixed response -- stable bone disease but new hepatic lesion identified. Bone scan: stable appearance of known osseous metastases, no convincing new bone lesions. CT: stable right iliac chain lymphadenopathy (1.0 cm, previously 1.1 cm). New finding: a 1.4 cm hypoenhancing lesion in segment VII of the liver, indeterminate but suspicious for metastatic disease. No pulmonary metastases. PSA at 6 months: 0.6 ng/mL (PSA nadir on olaparib). Impression: PSA and bone disease response with suspicious new hepatic lesion -- liver biopsy recommended to characterize.

At age 72 years, the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY -- CT-guided liver biopsy of segment VII hepatic lesion. Diagnosis: metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma. Immunohistochemistry: PSA positive (weak, focal), PSMA positive (strong), NKX3.1 positive, AMACR positive, CK7 negative, CK20 negative, TTF-1 negative. No neuroendocrine differentiation (synaptophysin negative, chromogranin negative, INSM1 negative). Proliferative index (Ki-67): 35% (high). Gleason-equivalent grade: high grade adenocarcinoma consistent with primary Gleason Grade Group 5 tumor. Impression: hepatic metastasis from primary prostatic adenocarcinoma without neuroendocrine transformation.

At age 72 years (7-8 months after olaparib initiation), the patient experienced recurrent transfusion-dependent anemia requiring a total of 4 additional red blood cell transfusion episodes (8 units pRBC cumulative over the course of olaparib therapy). Hemoglobin typically falling to 7.5-8.0 g/dL between transfusions approximately every 4-6 weeks. Olaparib continued at 200 mg BID as PSA and bone disease were controlled. Hematology consultation obtained: bone marrow biopsy performed, showing hypocellular marrow with erythroid hypoplasia consistent with PARP inhibitor toxicity; no evidence of marrow infiltration by metastatic prostate cancer (myelophthisic anemia excluded). Patient enrolled in a supportive care program including intravenous iron supplementation (ferric carboxymaltose) and nutritional optimization. Fatigue grade 2, limiting instrumental activities of daily living. ECOG PS 2.

At age 72 years (8 months after olaparib initiation), the patient had an oncologist office assessment showing PSA progression with confirmed visceral metastatic disease. PSA: 1.8 ng/mL (rising from nadir of 0.6 ng/mL, meeting PSA progression criteria with confirmed >25% rise and >2 ng/mL increase). Hemoglobin: 8.3 g/dL (post-transfusion day 14, transfusion-dependent). Patient reporting worsening fatigue, decreased appetite, 8 lb weight loss over 2 months, and increasing right upper quadrant discomfort. ECOG PS 2. Liver biopsy confirmed hepatic metastasis from prostate cancer without neuroendocrine transformation. Impression: olaparib resistance with visceral (hepatic) progression. PSA progression confirmed. Significant treatment-related morbidity from anemia. Plan: olaparib discontinued. Multidisciplinary discussion regarding next-line therapy options including cabazitaxel-based chemotherapy, lutetium-177 PSMA radioligand therapy (pending eligibility), and/or clinical trial enrollment. Palliative care consultation placed to optimize symptom management and goals of care discussion. Patient and family meeting scheduled.

At age 72 years (at time of olaparib discontinuation), the patient had a PSMA PET/CT (68Ga-PSMA-11) for assessment of lutetium-177 PSMA-617 eligibility, which showed widespread metastatic disease. Findings: multiple PSMA-avid bone metastases (T10, T12, L3, right posterior 6th rib, left iliac wing, right posterior iliac crest, new right femoral head lesion -- 7 bone lesions total). PSMA-avid right iliac chain lymphadenopathy (1.3 cm, SUVmax 6.1). PSMA-avid hepatic lesion in segment VII (SUVmax 8.4, 1.8 cm -- interval growth from 1.4 cm). No brain metastases on skull survey. No new pulmonary lesions. Tumor PSMA expression: high (SUVmax range 3.8-8.4 across lesions). Impression: PSMA-avid metastatic CRPC with bone, lymph node, and visceral (hepatic) metastases. High PSMA expression consistent with eligibility for lutetium-177 PSMA-617 therapy pending clinical criteria.

At age 72 years, the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board review and oncologist assessment following olaparib discontinuation. Discussed case at GU oncology tumor board. Patient has: (1) mCRPC with visceral (hepatic) progression after PARP inhibitor therapy for BRCA2-mutated disease, (2) prior treatments including ADT, abiraterone, definitive RT, enzalutamide, and olaparib, (3) significant comorbidity burden including transfusion-dependent anemia (ECOG PS 2), (4) PSMA-avid disease on recent PSMA PET/CT. Consensus recommendation: lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (177Lu-PSMA-617, Pluvicto) as next-line therapy given high PSMA expression, post-taxane-naive status (cabazitaxel not yet given), and PSMA eligibility criteria met. Note that VISION trial included patients with at least one prior ARSI and prior taxane, however given ECOG PS 2, significant anemia, and patient preference to avoid chemotherapy, multidisciplinary team agreed to proceed with lutetium-177 PSMA-617. Palliative care co-management in place. Discussion with patient regarding prognosis, treatment goals (disease control, quality of life), and symptom management. Patient opted to pursue active therapy with understanding of palliative intent.

GERMLINE GENETIC TESTING REPORT

---

Patient Name: Gerald Hutchinson

Sex: Male
Ordering Provider: [GU Medical Oncology]
Indication: Castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC); strong family history of prostate cancer (father and paternal uncle); high-risk disease features at diagnosis (Gleason 9, PSA 87.4 ng/mL, cT3bN1M0)

SPECIMEN INFORMATION
Specimen Type: Peripheral whole blood
Specimen ID: [LAB ID ON FILE]
Volume Received: 8.0 mL (adequate)
Specimen Condition: Acceptable
Date Accessioned: [on file]
Date Reported: [on file]
Testing Laboratory: Myriad Genetic Laboratories, Inc., Salt Lake City, UT (CLIA #: 42D0917589)

---

SUMMARY OF RESULTS

PATHOGENIC VARIANT DETECTED

Gene: BRCA2
Variant: c.5946delT (p.Ser1982Argfs*22)
Classification: PATHOGENIC
Inheritance: Autosomal dominant
Associated Hereditary Syndrome: Hereditary Breast and Ovarian Cancer Syndrome (HBOC); Hereditary Prostate Cancer Susceptibility

---

DETAILED VARIANT DESCRIPTION

BRCA2 — PATHOGENIC
Nucleotide Change: c.5946delT
Amino Acid Change: p.Ser1982Argfs*22
Variant Type: Frameshift (single nucleotide deletion)
Exon: Exon 11 of BRCA2
Zygosity: Germline (heterozygous by germline testing; somatic tumor specimen not evaluated in this assay)
Expected Functional Consequence: Loss of function. The single-nucleotide deletion introduces a frameshift beginning at codon 1982, replacing serine (Ser) with arginine (Arg) and generating a premature termination codon at position 22 of the new reading frame (p.Ser1982Argfs*22). The resulting truncated protein is predicted to be subject to nonsense-mediated mRNA decay and/or produce a non-functional protein lacking critical C-terminal DNA-binding and repair domains of BRCA2, including the BRC repeats required for RAD51 interaction and homologous recombination.
dbSNP ID: rs80359550
ClinVar Accession: RCV000031443 (classified Pathogenic by multiple submitters)
Classification Basis: This variant has been previously reported in multiple individuals and families with BRCA2-associated cancers (breast, ovarian, pancreatic, and prostate). Functional studies demonstrate markedly reduced BRCA2 protein expression and impaired homologous recombination repair capacity. The variant is absent from population frequency databases (gnomAD v2.1.1 and v3.1.2) at appreciable frequency (allele frequency <0.0001). It is classified as Pathogenic by ACMG/AMP criteria (PVS1, PS1, PM2, PP5).

---

ADDITIONAL GENES EVALUATED — NO PATHOGENIC OR LIKELY PATHOGENIC VARIANTS DETECTED

The following genes were sequenced and analyzed in full by next-generation sequencing with deletion/duplication analysis. All results below are NEGATIVE (no pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants identified):

BRCA1: No pathogenic variant detected. Sequence analysis and large rearrangement analysis negative.
ATM: No pathogenic variant detected.
PALB2: No pathogenic variant detected.
CHEK2: No pathogenic variant detected.
BRIP1: No pathogenic variant detected.
NBN: No pathogenic variant detected.
RAD51C: No pathogenic variant detected.
RAD51D: No pathogenic variant detected.
MSH2: No pathogenic variant detected.
MLH1: No pathogenic variant detected.
MSH6: No pathogenic variant detected.
PMS2: No pathogenic variant detected.
EPCAM: No pathogenic variant detected (large deletion analysis performed; negative).
APC: No pathogenic variant detected.
MUTYH: No pathogenic variant detected. (Bi-allelic founder variants Y179C and G396D: not detected.)
TP53: No pathogenic variant detected.
PTEN: No pathogenic variant detected.
CDH1: No pathogenic variant detected.
STK11: No pathogenic variant detected.
BMPR1A: No pathogenic variant detected.
SMAD4: No pathogenic variant detected.
HOXB13: Variant G84E (c.251G>A): NOT DETECTED.

Variants of Uncertain Significance (VUS): None identified.

---

METHODOLOGY

Next-generation sequencing (NGS) was performed on genomic DNA extracted from the submitted peripheral blood specimen. Library preparation was performed using a custom hybridization capture panel targeting coding exons and flanking intronic sequences of all 25 genes included in the myRisk panel. Sequencing was performed on an Illumina platform with a minimum read depth of 50x across all targeted regions (mean depth >250x). Variant calls were filtered and classified using Myriad's proprietary bioinformatics pipeline. Large genomic rearrangements (deletions and duplications spanning one or more exons) were assessed using a validated read-depth and allele-ratio algorithm. Mosaic variants with allele fractions below 20% may not be reliably detected by this assay. Intronic, deep promoter, and synonymous variants without predicted impact on splicing are not reported. Somatic mosaicism and copy-neutral loss of heterozygosity at germline variant loci cannot be distinguished from true heterozygosity by this assay.

---

INTERPRETATION

This patient carries a confirmed germline pathogenic frameshift variant in BRCA2 (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22). BRCA2 encodes a tumor suppressor protein essential for high-fidelity DNA double-strand break repair via homologous recombination. Germline loss-of-function variants in BRCA2 confer substantially elevated lifetime risks for multiple malignancies, including prostate cancer. In the context of prostate cancer specifically:

- Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variants are associated with significantly increased prostate cancer risk (estimated lifetime risk 15-25% vs. ~10-12% in the general male population) and with earlier onset, higher Gleason grade, more advanced stage at presentation, and inferior oncologic outcomes compared to non-carriers.
- BRCA2-associated prostate cancers frequently exhibit biallelic inactivation of BRCA2 in tumor tissue (via somatic loss of heterozygosity, somatic point mutation, or promoter methylation affecting the second allele), resulting in homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) and consequent sensitivity to PARP inhibitors.
- The germline variant identified here is consistent with the patient's clinical features at diagnosis (Gleason 9, seminal vesicle invasion, nodal involvement) and his family history of prostate cancer in a first-degree and second-degree relative on the paternal side.

Familial implications: Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variants are autosomal dominant with 50% probability of transmission to first-degree relatives. Cascade genetic testing is indicated for adult first-degree relatives (children, siblings). Female relatives carrying this variant face significantly elevated risks for breast and ovarian cancer and should be referred for risk-appropriate cancer surveillance and/or risk reduction discussions.

This report was generated under CLIA-certified laboratory conditions. Results should be interpreted in the context of personal and family medical history. This report does not constitute clinical management guidance.

Patient Name: Gerald Hutchinson
Date of Birth: [on file]
Sex: Male
Ordering Physician: [GU Oncology Attending]
Institution: [Academic Medical Center GU Oncology]
Clinical Diagnosis: Castration-resistant prostate adenocarcinoma (mCRPC); germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant known
Specimen ID: FM-PRCA-[XXXXXXXX]
Specimen Type: Formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue -- prostate needle core biopsy (re-biopsy at time of castration resistance)
Biopsy Site: Prostate (primary site re-biopsy, transrectal ultrasound-guided, left base)
Date of Procedure: [see clinical record]
Tumor Nuclei Percentage (Pathologist Estimate): 60%
Sequencing Platform: Hybrid-capture based next-generation sequencing; all classes of genomic alterations assessed
Genes Analyzed: 324 cancer-related genes (full coding sequence), select introns for rearrangement detection, whole-genome copy number analysis
Mean Coverage Depth: 724x (tumor); 471x (matched blood normal)
FDA-Approved Indication: FoundationOne CDx is an FDA-approved companion diagnostic for multiple tumor types including prostate cancer
Quality Control: PASS

--- GENOMIC FINDINGS SUMMARY ---

SHORT VARIANTS (SUBSTITUTIONS / INSERTIONS / DELETIONS)

1. BRCA2 p.Ser1982Argfs*22 (c.5946delT)
   Variant Class: Short variant -- frameshift deletion
   Allele Frequency: 93%
   Classification: PATHOGENIC
   Notes: This frameshift variant results in a premature stop codon at amino acid position 2003, predicting loss of the C-terminal BRCA2 functional domain including the RAD51-binding repeats (BRC repeats 5-8) and the C-terminal DNA binding domain. This variant is concordant with the germline pathogenic variant previously identified on Myriad myRisk testing (germline BRCA2 c.5946delT). Loss of heterozygosity (LOH) is confirmed at this locus by copy number analysis (see below), establishing biallelic inactivation. This finding is consistent with the "two-hit" model of tumor suppressor inactivation and constitutes a somatic LOH event at the BRCA2 locus in the context of a germline heterozygous carrier. BRCA2 biallelic loss confers sensitivity to PARP inhibitors (ola, Rubraca; FDA-approved for mCRPC) and platinum-based chemotherapy.

2. TP53 p.R175H (c.524G>A)
   Variant Class: Short variant -- missense substitution
   Allele Frequency: 48%
   Classification: PATHOGENIC
   Notes: R175H is among the most common and well-characterized gain-of-function TP53 hotspot mutations in human cancer. Arginine-to-histidine substitution at codon 175 disrupts the DNA-contact and structural integrity of the p53 protein, abrogating its function as a tumor suppressor and conferring oncogenic gain-of-function activities including promotion of invasion, drug resistance, and altered metabolism. This mutation is present at ~50% allele frequency, consistent with hemizygous loss (one allele mutated, second allele subject to LOH on the 17p arm). In prostate cancer, TP53 alteration is associated with more aggressive disease biology and resistance to AR-directed therapies. No co-occurring MDM2 amplification detected.

COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS (CNAs)

3. AR Amplification
   Locus: Xq12
   Copy Number: 8 copies (baseline diploid reference)
   Classification: AMPLIFICATION
   Notes: Androgen receptor gene amplification is among the most common resistance mechanisms in CRPC, detected in approximately 30-60% of CRPC specimens. At 8 copies, this represents high-level amplification. AR amplification leads to elevated AR protein expression, enabling sustained AR signaling even at castrate testosterone levels, effectively bypassing hormonal suppression. This mechanism underlies resistance to androgen deprivation therapy and is associated with reduced benefit from second-generation AR-signaling inhibitors (Xtandi, abi). Importantly, no AR point mutations conferring ligand-binding domain alterations (T878A, W742C, F877L, L702H) were detected in this specimen. The amplification in isolation, without a sensitizing AR mutation, may indicate that AR pathway activity is maintained through overexpression rather than altered ligand specificity.

4. RB1 Deep Deletion
   Locus: 13q14.2
   Copy Number: 0 (homozygous deletion)
   Classification: LOSS -- DEEP DELETION
   Notes: Homozygous deletion of the RB1 tumor suppressor locus. RB1 encodes the retinoblastoma protein, a critical cell cycle checkpoint regulator at the G1/S boundary. Biallelic RB1 loss is associated with aggressive prostate cancer biology including accelerated cell cycle progression, resistance to AR-pathway inhibitors, and phenotypic plasticity -- including increased risk of neuroendocrine or small cell transformation over time. The absence of neuroendocrine differentiation on the current biopsy (synaptophysin, chromogranin, INSM1 not assessed on this NGS specimen but negative on prior TRUS biopsy IHC) is noted; however, RB1 loss is a genomic risk factor for this transformation and warrants monitoring.

5. PTEN Homozygous Deletion
   Locus: 10q23.31
   Copy Number: 0 (homozygous deletion)
   Classification: LOSS -- DEEP DELETION
   Notes: Biallelic loss of the PTEN tumor suppressor gene. PTEN is the major negative regulator of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway; its loss results in constitutive activation of this pro-survival axis. PTEN loss occurs in approximately 20-30% of localized prostate cancers and in up to 40-50% of CRPC specimens. Combined PTEN loss and AR amplification represent co-occurring alterations that together drive robust tumor cell survival through parallel signaling pathways (AR axis and PI3K/AKT). Note: PIK3CA mutation was not detected (see below); PTEN loss is the primary PI3K-pathway activation mechanism in this tumor. PTEN loss is associated with aggressive disease and reduced benefit from AR-pathway inhibition.

6. MYC Amplification
   Locus: 8q24.21
   Copy Number: 4 copies (low-level amplification / gain)
   Classification: AMPLIFICATION (LOW-LEVEL)
   Notes: Low-level MYC amplification at 8q24, a common copy number event in prostate cancer occurring at increased frequency in CRPC. MYC drives global transcriptional amplification, metabolic reprogramming, and cell cycle progression. In the context of co-occurring AR amplification, MYC gain may cooperate to sustain tumor cell proliferation.

7. CDK4 Copy Number Gain
   Locus: 12q14.1
   Copy Number: 3 copies (low-level gain)
   Classification: GAIN
   Notes: Low-level CDK4 gain. CDK4 is a cell cycle kinase that, in complex with cyclin D, phosphorylates and inactivates RB1 to promote S-phase entry. In this tumor, CDK4 gain combined with deep RB1 deletion creates a context where the canonical CDK4-mediated growth suppressive checkpoint is entirely abrogated (RB1 protein absent). CDK4/6 inhibitors (Ibrance, ribo, abema) have limited rationale in the setting of biallelic RB1 loss.

TIER I -- FDA-APPROVED THERAPIES WITH COMPANION DIAGNOSTIC INDICATION IN THIS TUMOR TYPE:

  BRCA2 biallelic loss (germline + somatic LOH)
  - ola (Lynparza, AstraZeneca/MSD): FDA-approved for mCRPC in patients with deleterious or suspected deleterious germline or somatic BRCA1/2 mutations, based on PROfound trial. FoundationOne CDx is the FDA-approved companion diagnostic for this indication. APPLICABLE.

  Note: ola is preferred over Rubraca based on more mature PROfound data and broader clinical adoption.

TIER II -- LIKELY CLINICALLY RELEVANT / POTENTIAL RESISTANCE MECHANISMS:

  AR Amplification (8x)
  - High-level AR amplification is an established mechanism of resistance to AR-signaling inhibitors (Xtandi, Zytiga, darolutamide, Erleada). Presence of AR amplification at CRPC transition is consistent with selection during prior ADT plus Zytiga and is expected to confer reduced efficacy of enza or other ARSI as subsequent therapy.
  - No AR ligand binding domain point mutations detected; resistance mechanism appears to be amplification-driven overexpression rather than altered ligand specificity.

  PTEN Loss (homozygous deletion) + PI3K/AKT pathway activation
  - Combined PTEN loss and AR amplification: PTEN loss may predict primary or secondary resistance to AR-pathway inhibitors. In the context of mCRPC, PTEN loss has been associated with reduced rPFS on Xtandi (AFFIRM/PREVAIL subset analyses). AKT inhibitors (ipatasertib, capivasertib) in combination with Xtandi have been evaluated in mCRPC with PTEN loss (IPATential150 trial); capivasertib plus Xtandi (CAPItello-281) is an emerging therapeutic option for PTEN-deficient CRPC.
  - PTEN/AKT pathway: no currently FDA-approved AKT inhibitor for prostate cancer at time of this report, but clinical trial eligibility should be considered.

  RB1 Loss (homozygous deletion)
  - Biallelic RB1 loss is associated with AR pathway independence, cell-cycle dysregulation, and increased risk of lineage plasticity / neuroendocrine transformation over time.
  - Clinical implication: heightened surveillance for neuroendocrine differentiation on subsequent biopsies and/or serum markers (chromogranin A, NSE). Platinum-VP-16 chemotherapy may be appropriate if neuroendocrine transformation occurs.
  - CDK4/6 inhibitors: NOT recommended given biallelic RB1 loss (loss of the drug target's downstream effector).

  TP53 p.R175H (gain-of-function)
  - Gain-of-function TP53 mutation associated with chemotherapy resistance and aggressive tumor biology. No currently FDA-approved targeted therapy directed at p53 R175H in prostate cancer. Clinical trial enrollment including p53-directed strategies (APR-246/eprenetapopt, PRIMA-1Met) may be considered in later lines.

TIER III -- BIOMARKERS WITHOUT CURRENTLY MATCHED THERAPY (INVESTIGATIONAL CONTEXT):
  - MYC amplification: No direct therapeutic target; relevant as a marker of aggressive biology.
  - CDK4 gain: CDK4/6 inhibitor therapy not applicable given co-occurring biallelic RB1 loss.

Gerald Hutchinson is a 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC, BRCA2 germline pathogenic variant, AR-amplified by somatic NGS) presenting today for a scheduled 9-month follow-up on Xtandi. HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Hutchinson was originally diagnosed four years ago with high-risk locally advanced prostate cancer (cT3bN1M0, Gleason 4+5=9, Grade Group 5, PSA 87.4 ng/mL) and was treated with combined ADT plus abi/prednisone and definitive IMRT (78 Gy to the prostate and seminal vesicles with concurrent pelvic nodal coverage). He achieved a PSA nadir of 0.2 ng/mL. Approximately 12 months ago he was found to have biochemical castration resistance with a PSA doubling time of roughly 3.5 months while on ADT plus abi; confirmatory castrate testosterone at that time was 9 ng/dL. Germline testing revealed a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift variant (c.5946delT), and somatic Foundation One CDx testing on re-biopsy at CRPC transition demonstrated biallelic BRCA2 loss, AR amplification (copy number 8x), TP53 R175H, RB1 deep deletion, PTEN homozygous deletion, and elevated gLOH at 18%. PSMA PET/CT performed at time of CRPC showed a PSMA-avid right iliac chain node and a single focus of uptake at the right posterior iliac crest suspicious for early osseous metastasis not detected on conventional bone scan.

Given prior Zytiga exposure, AR amplification without a known resistance point mutation (no T878A, no F877L), and a desire to hold Lynparza in reserve given concerns about tolerability and optimal sequencing, enza 160 mg orally daily was initiated approximately 9 months ago with continuation of leup for castrate maintenance. abi was discontinued at that time.

His early course on enza was encouraging. At the 3-month mark, PSA had declined to 0.9 ng/mL (61% reduction from 2.3 ng/mL baseline), and he reported only grade 1 fatigue and mild cognitive fogginess without falls or seizures. Restaging bone scan and CT at 6 months showed stable appearance of the known right posterior iliac crest lesion, no new bone lesions, and no visceral metastases; PSA at that time was 0.7 ng/mL, his Xtandi nadir.

Since that nadir, however, PSA has been rising. Serial values over the past approximately 3 months read as follows: 0.7 ng/mL (nadir, at 6-month mark), 1.4 ng/mL (7.5 months), and now 2.8 ng/mL at today's visit. PSA doubling time calculated from these three data points is in the range of 5-6 weeks, which is rapid.

Additionally, Mr. Hutchinson notes new onset of dull, aching mid-back pain, rated 3/10 at rest and worsening to 5-6/10 with prolonged standing or walking. He has been self-managing with acetaminophen with partial relief. He denies radicular symptoms, lower extremity weakness or numbness, bowel or bladder dysfunction, or saddle anesthesia. He reports worsening fatigue over the past month, which he describes as a heavier, more pervasive tiredness compared to his prior fatigue on enza. He has also noted mild decreased appetite but no significant weight loss. No fevers, chills, or night sweats.

His hypertension and type 2 diabetes remain managed with his primary care provider. He remains functionally independent at home, living with his wife, though he notes difficulty with some household tasks due to fatigue.

ANCILLARY STUDIES -- IMMUNOHISTOCHEMISTRY:

IHC performed on representative positive cores (block D, left base; block L, targeted left base biopsy):

  PSA (prostate-specific antigen): POSITIVE -- diffuse cytoplasmic staining, moderate to strong intensity. Confirms prostatic origin.

  PSMA (prostate-specific membrane antigen): POSITIVE -- strong, diffuse membranous and cytoplasmic staining. Intensity: 3+ across tumor cells.

  NKX3.1 (prostate-specific transcription factor): POSITIVE -- strong nuclear staining in tumor cells. Consistent with acinar adenocarcinoma of prostatic origin.

  AMACR (alpha-methylacyl-CoA racemase, P504S): POSITIVE -- strong granular cytoplasmic staining. Consistent with adenocarcinoma.

  p63 (basal cell marker): NEGATIVE in tumor glands. Absence of basal cell layer confirms invasive acinar adenocarcinoma (no high-grade PIN only).

  CK5/6 (basal cell marker): NEGATIVE in tumor glands. Consistent with invasive adenocarcinoma.

  Synaptophysin: NEGATIVE. No neuroendocrine differentiation.

  Chromogranin A: NEGATIVE. No neuroendocrine differentiation.

  CD56 (NCAM): NEGATIVE. No neuroendocrine differentiation.

  Ki-67 (MIB-1 proliferative index): Approximately 28% of tumor nuclei positive (high proliferative rate, consistent with Grade Group 5 disease).

  ERG (ETS-related gene, TMPRSS2-ERG fusion surrogate): NEGATIVE. No ERG protein overexpression detected. ERG-negative, Gleason 4+5 disease.

Interpretation: The morphological and immunophenotypic profile is diagnostic of high-grade acinar adenocarcinoma of prostatic origin, Gleason score 4+5=9, Grade Group 5. ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Hutchinson is a 71-year-old male with mCRPC harboring germline and somatic biallelic BRCA2 loss (confirmed by germline testing and Foundation One CDx with LOH), now 2 months into treatment with olaparib 300 mg BID. He is demonstrating a strong and gratifying PSA response, with a 61% decline from 2.8 ng/mL to 1.1 ng/mL -- this degree of early PSA fall is highly consistent with on-target PARP inhibitor activity in a BRCA2-deficient tumor and tracks well with what we observe in the PROfound trial cohort.

The primary concern today is the declining hemoglobin: from 11.8 g/dL at baseline to 10.4 g/dL now, representing a 1.4 g/dL drop over 8 weeks. The anemia is normocytic, with a borderline reticulocyte index suggesting a hypoproliferative pattern rather than hemolysis or blood loss. Iron studies do not support iron deficiency as a primary driver. This picture is most consistent with early olaparib-related marrow suppression (erythroid toxicity is a well-described class effect of PARP inhibitors, occurring in roughly 20-25% of patients on olaparib and sometimes necessitating dose reduction or transfusion). At this point, the hemoglobin is 10.4 g/dL -- he is not acutely symptomatic from anemia (no dyspnea, no lightheadedness), so I will hold on transfusion and dose reduction for now. However, this will require close monitoring as there is a real risk of progression to symptomatic, transfusion-requiring anemia if the marrow suppression worsens.

Case presented at GU oncology multidisciplinary tumor board. Attendees included medical oncology, radiation oncology, interventional radiology, palliative care, and urologic oncology. Key clinical points discussed:

5. Options considered: (a) cabazitaxel-based chemotherapy -- active regimen but ECOG PS 2, transfusion-dependence, and patient preference to defer chemotherapy; (b) lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (Pluvicto) -- PSMA PET eligible, ARSI-exposed, taxane-naive (note VISION trial required prior taxane, however this patient has significant comorbidity limiting chemotherapy and PSMA eligibility is robust); (c) clinical trial -- no currently open GU trials for which patient is eligible; (d) best supportive care alone -- discussed but patient wishes to continue active treatment.

Consensus recommendation: Proceed with lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (Pluvicto) radioligand therapy. Acknowledge deviation from VISION trial eligibility (taxane-naive), but team consensus that benefit/risk favors this approach given high PSMA expression, declining performance status, transfusion-dependent anemia precluding safe chemotherapy delivery, and patient values.

DIAGNOSTIC FINDINGS

SPECIMEN A: CT-guided core needle biopsy, liver segment VII

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:
Sections from all three cores demonstrate infiltration of the hepatic parenchyma by a malignant epithelial neoplasm. The tumor cells are arranged in poorly cohesive nests, small acinar structures, and occasional single-file cords within a desmoplastic stroma. Individual tumor cells are polygonal with moderate to abundant pale eosinophilic to amphophilic cytoplasm. Nuclei are enlarged, pleomorphic, and hyperchromatic with conspicuous nucleoli. Mitotic figures are readily identified (estimated 3-4/10 HPF). There is no glandular luminal differentiation in the majority of the specimen; the few acinar structures present show compressed irregular lumina without mucinous content.

The surrounding hepatic parenchyma shows mild steatosis and portal tract expansion with mild chronic inflammation, consistent with reactive changes. No bile duct proliferation or significant fibrosis noted in uninvolved liver parenchyma. No normal prostate tissue is present.

The morphology is most consistent with a high-grade carcinoma with features favoring metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma, given the clinical history. The tumor does NOT demonstrate features of small cell or neuroendocrine carcinoma (no nuclear molding, no salt-and-pepper chromatin, no geographic necrosis, no Azzopardi effect). No squamous differentiation identified.

   -- NO NEUROENDOCRINE DIFFERENTIATION IDENTIFIED
   -- NO TREATMENT-EMERGENT SMALL CELL CARCINOMA
   -- SEE COMMENT

COMMENT:
This hepatic core biopsy demonstrates infiltration by high-grade carcinoma with an immunophenotype diagnostic of metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma. The tumor retains expression of PSMA, NKX3.1, and AMACR, confirming prostatic lineage. PSA expression is reduced (weak, focal), a finding consistent with disease progression and/or prior exposure to androgen signaling inhibitors (abiraterone, enzalutamide), which are known to suppress PSA protein expression independently of androgen receptor activity.

Importantly, neuroendocrine differentiation is absent by morphology and a comprehensive IHC panel (synaptophysin, chromogranin A, INSM1, CD56 all negative). Treatment-emergent neuroendocrine prostate cancer (t-NEPC), which can arise in the setting of prolonged androgen pathway suppression, is not identified in this specimen. Correlation with clinical and imaging findings is recommended.

The Ki-67 proliferative index of approximately 35% is elevated and consistent with the high-grade nature of the primary tumor (Gleason Grade Group 5, Gleason 9).

Androgen receptor expression is maintained in the majority of tumor cells (AR positive, ~70%), which may have implications for ongoing AR-directed therapeutic strategies in this case.

Correlation with prior tissue diagnosis, NGS results, and clinical context is recommended for therapeutic decision-making.

Briefly, following diagnosis, he was initiated on androgen deprivation therapy with leuprolide (Lupron) plus abiraterone (Zytiga) with prednisone, achieving a PSA nadir of 0.4 ng/mL. He received definitive IMRT to the prostate and pelvis (78 Gy / 39 fractions to the primary, 54 Gy to the pelvic nodes), completing approximately 8 weeks of radiation with acceptable acute toxicity (grade 1-2 urinary frequency, mild rectal irritation). Mr. Hutchinson is a 72-year-old male with mCRPC, germline and somatic BRCA2 loss, who has progressed through multiple lines of therapy including ADT + abiraterone (with definitive RT), enzalutamide, and olaparib, now with confirmed visceral (hepatic) metastases, widespread PSMA-avid osseous disease, and significant treatment-related morbidity (transfusion-dependent anemia, ECOG PS 2). Case reviewed and discussed at GU Oncology Multidisciplinary Tumor Board today.

1. mCRPC -- Progression on olaparib with visceral metastasis / Next-line therapy
Following multidisciplinary tumor board discussion, the recommended next-line therapy is lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (Pluvicto, 177Lu-PSMA-617). The basis for this recommendation:
- Confirmed high PSMA expression on 68Ga-PSMA-11 PET/CT across all disease sites (SUVmax 3.8-8.4), satisfying expression threshold for radioligand therapy
- Three prior lines of systemic therapy in CRPC setting (abiraterone, enzalutamide, olaparib)
- Patient has not received taxane-based chemotherapy; cabazitaxel was discussed at length but patient declines cytotoxic chemotherapy at this time, which is his right given the palliative intent of all therapy at this stage
- Significant comorbidities limiting chemotherapy tolerance: ECOG PS 2, transfusion-dependent anemia, hepatic metastases
- PSMA-based radioligand therapy (VISION trial regimen): 7.4 GBq (200 mCi) IV q6 weeks x 6 cycles. Patient was counseled on mechanism of action, expected response rates (PSA response in ~46% per VISION, OS benefit), potential side effects (fatigue, dry mouth/xerostomia, nausea, hematologic toxicity -- particularly relevant given his pre-existing anemia), and radiation safety precautions
- Pre-treatment requirements: creatinine/renal function adequate (eGFR ~66), no salivary gland exclusion criteria, no bowel obstruction
- Will coordinate with nuclear medicine / theranostics program for treatment scheduling
- Referral to nuclear medicine / Pluvicto treatment program placed today
- ADT (leuprolide) to continue throughout -- testosterone must remain castrate

2. Anemia -- transfusion-dependent, olaparib-related
Hemoglobin 8.3 g/dL post-transfusion approximately 2 weeks ago. Olaparib now discontinued, which should allow marrow recovery over the coming weeks. Will monitor CBC weekly x 4 weeks then biweekly. Anticipate slow hematopoietic recovery given marrow biopsy-confirmed erythroid hypoplasia. Transfusion threshold: maintain Hgb >8.0 g/dL; if symptomatic or Hgb <7.5, transfuse 1-2 units pRBC. Continuing IV iron supplementation (ferric carboxymaltose). EPO-stimulating agents remain deferred; with PSMA-targeted therapy imminent, will reassess in collaboration with hematology. Continue denosumab with calcium/vitamin D.

3. Bone metastases / skeletal health
Patient has 7 known osseous metastases. Post-T10 RT with pain response, no new focal bony pain today. L3 involvement -- no neurological compromise at this time. Continue denosumab 120 mg SQ q4 weeks. Counsel patient on signs/symptoms of spinal cord compression and instructed to go to ER immediately with any new leg weakness, numbness, bowel/bladder incontinence. No new RT indicated at this time.

4. Hepatic metastasis
Biopsy-confirmed hepatic met, segment VII (1.8 cm on most recent PSMA PET/CT). Mildly elevated LFTs (AST 44, ALT 38, ALP 112) likely attributable. No current signs of hepatic decompensation (bilirubin normal, INR normal, albumin only mildly low). Will monitor LFTs at each visit. If hepatic disease progresses on lutetium-177, will revisit options including consideration of liver-directed approaches in consultation with IR/surgical oncology, though this remains investigational in the prostate context.

5. Nutrition / Performance status / Symptom management
8-10 lb weight loss over 2 months, albumin 3.4. Referral to oncology dietitian placed. Patient offered nutritional supplementation (Ensure, Boost). Ondansetron continued PRN nausea. Palliative care team actively co-managing -- ongoing goals of care and symptom management. Discussed prognosis honestly with patient and wife in the context of mCRPC with visceral metastases and prior multi-line therapy. Patient understands the disease is not curable but expresses strong wish to pursue further treatment for disease control and quality of life maintenance. Goals are aligned with disease-directed palliative therapy.

6. Diabetes / Hypertension
Continue metformin and lisinopril. Blood pressure well controlled today. Glucose management: will continue to monitor; note that prednisone being weaned (was co-administered with abiraterone, should no longer be required), which may help with glucose control.

7. Prednisone taper
Prednisone 5 mg BID was co-prescribed with abiraterone. Abiraterone was stopped at CRPC progression months ago. Taper prednisone: 5 mg daily x 2 weeks, then discontinue. Patient counseled on signs of adrenal insufficiency (unlikely given short course, but to be aware).

8. Follow-up
Return to clinic in 3-4 weeks, ideally following scheduling of Pluvicto initiation with nuclear medicine. CBC to be checked at 2-week interval. PSA, CMP, LFTs at next visit. Patient instructed to call with worsening pain, new neurologic symptoms, shortness of breath, or if requiring transfusion before scheduled visit.

Total face-to-face time with patient and family: approximately 55 minutes (>50% time in counseling and coordination of care).

TECHNIQUE: Following intravenous administration of 68Ga-PSMA-11 (185 MBq, confirmed activity), the patient rested comfortably for a 60-minute uptake period. Dedicated skull survey images were reviewed for intracranial involvement. Images reviewed on dedicated workstation with multiplanar reconstruction in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes.

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK:
No abnormal PSMA-avid foci within the calvarium, skull base, or intracranial compartment. No cervical lymphadenopathy. No supraclavicular foci of uptake. Salivary gland and lacrimal gland physiologic uptake noted bilaterally, as expected with this tracer.

CHEST:
Lungs: No pulmonary nodules or masses with appreciable PSMA avidity. No pleural effusion. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy with pathologic PSMA uptake.

Bone (thoracic):
-- T10 vertebral body: Areas of sclerotic change corresponding to the previously irradiated metastatic lesion (30 Gy in 10 fractions, completed approximately 8 months prior). Post-radiation marrow changes are present.
-- T12 vertebral body: New compared to prior PSMA PET/CT. Focal PSMA-avid lesion in the left posterolateral T12 vertebral body, SUVmax 5.7, without cortical breakthrough or associated soft tissue mass. Not clearly seen on the prior bone scan performed approximately 2 months ago.
-- Right posterior 6th rib: Persistent PSMA-avid focus at the posterolateral right 6th rib, SUVmax 3.9. Previously noted on conventional bone scan approximately 6 months prior; stable in size and tracer avidity compared to that time.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: A 1.8 cm hypoenhancing lesion in hepatic segment VII demonstrates intense PSMA avidity (SUVmax 8.4). This is interval growth from the 1.4 cm lesion documented on CT approximately 8 weeks ago and confirmed as metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma on CT-guided core biopsy. No additional hepatic lesions are identified with confidence above background, though a subtle 0.5 cm area of mildly elevated uptake in segment III is noted (SUVmax 2.2) and is nonspecific -- may reflect very early micro-metastatic involvement versus background; correlation with follow-up imaging recommended.

Retroperitoneum / Lymph nodes: No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy above the level of the aortic bifurcation with convincing PSMA avidity. Physiologic renal, hepatic, and splenic uptake noted.

Prostate bed / Pelvis:
-- Right iliac chain: The previously identified right iliac chain lymph node demonstrates persistent and now somewhat more prominent PSMA avidity (node measures approximately 1.3 cm on CT, SUVmax 6.1), representing interval increase in metabolic activity compared to the prior PSMA PET/CT where SUVmax was 4.2. -- Prostate bed: Heterogeneous post-treatment changes without a discrete PSMA-avid soft tissue mass to suggest gross local recurrence.

Bone (lumbar spine and pelvis):
-- L3 vertebral body: PSMA-avid lesion in the right hemivertebra of L3 (SUVmax 5.2). Previously identified on MRI spine as a focus of early marrow signal change.
-- Right posterior iliac crest: Persistent PSMA-avid osseous lesion (SUVmax 4.9), the index lesion first identified on PSMA PET/CT approximately 24 months ago. Modestly increased uptake compared to prior PET, consistent with ongoing disease activity.
-- Left iliac wing: Previously identified on conventional bone scan approximately 6 months prior. PSMA-avid focus at the left anterior iliac wing (SUVmax 4.4), corresponding to a subtle sclerotic focus on CT.
-- Right femoral head: New compared to all prior imaging. Focal PSMA-avid lesion in the right femoral head/neck junction (SUVmax 3.8), without cortical involvement or fracture risk feature on CT review. This represents a new site of osseous metastatic disease not identified on the bone scan performed approximately 2 months ago.

IMPRESSION:

1. Extensive PSMA-avid metastatic castration-resistant prostatic adenocarcinoma involving multiple anatomic compartments, representing significant interval progression compared to PSMA PET/CT performed approximately 24 months ago.

2. Osseous disease: Seven discrete sites of PSMA-avid bone involvement identified -- T10 vertebral body (previously irradiated, residual uptake), T12 vertebral body (new), right posterior 6th rib (stable), L3 vertebral body (previously seen on MRI), right posterior iliac crest (index lesion, slight increase in uptake), left anterior iliac wing (previously seen on bone scan), and right femoral head/neck junction (new). The right femoral head lesion is a new finding and warrants orthopedic surgery or bone health clinic awareness given weight-bearing location, though no fracture or cortical breach is identified at this time.

3. Nodal disease: Right iliac chain nodal metastasis with increased PSMA avidity compared to prior. Additional small satellite right external iliac chain foci are noted with low-level uptake, possible early nodal involvement.

4. Visceral disease: PSMA-avid hepatic segment VII metastasis (SUVmax 8.4, 1.8 cm), with high tracer expression. A second small indeterminate focus in segment III (SUVmax 2.2) is nonspecific and requires follow-up. The segment VII lesion was biopsy-confirmed as prostatic adenocarcinoma without neuroendocrine differentiation.

5. PSMA expression assessment: All identified metastatic sites demonstrate meaningful PSMA avidity (SUVmax range 3.8 to 8.4 across lesions). No lesion with appreciable FDG-avid, PSMA-low phenotype is identified to suggest significant neuroendocrine dedifferentiation or PSMA-low subclonal escape. These findings are consistent with high and relatively uniform PSMA expression across the metastatic burden. From a tracer-uptake standpoint, the tumor PSMA expression profile would appear favorable for consideration of PSMA-directed radioligand therapy, though clinical eligibility determination per institutional criteria is at the discretion of the treating team.

6. No intracranial metastases. No leptomeningeal disease. No cord compression identified, though a dedicated MRI spine study (most recently performed approximately 6 months ago) should be considered for updated neurological risk assessment given new T12 involvement.

MULTIPARAMETRIC MRI PROSTATE AND PELVIS WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 71-year-old male with newly diagnosed prostatic adenocarcinoma on TRUS-guided biopsy (Gleason 4+5=9, 10/12 cores positive). PSA 87.4 ng/mL. Referred for local staging and evaluation of regional nodal disease.

TECHNIQUE: Multiparametric MRI of the prostate and pelvis performed on a 3.0 Tesla scanner with endorectal coil and phased-array surface coil. Sequences obtained: axial and coronal T2-weighted imaging of the prostate and pelvis; diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping (b-values 0, 800, 1400 s/mm2); dynamic contrast-enhanced (DCE) sequences using intravenous gadolinium (Gadavist, 0.1 mmol/kg). No prior prostate MRI available for comparison.

---

FINDINGS:

PROSTATE:
The prostate gland is markedly enlarged, estimated total volume approximately 58 cc. There is a dominant, poorly marginated hypointense mass on T2-weighted imaging centered at the bilateral mid-gland and extending into the right apex, occupying the majority of the peripheral zone bilaterally. The dominant lesion measures approximately 3.4 cm in greatest dimension. The lesion demonstrates markedly restricted diffusion with very low ADC values (estimated ADC nadir in the range of 550 mm2/s) consistent with highly cellular carcinoma. Dynamic contrast enhancement reveals early arterial wash-in with rapid washout, characteristic of aggressive malignancy. Overall PI-RADS assessment category: 5 (clinically significant cancer very likely present). The central gland shows heterogeneous benign prostatic hyperplasia-type changes.

EXTRACAPSULAR EXTENSION (ECE):
There is unequivocal extracapsular extension at the right apical prostate, with irregular contour bulging and obliteration of the rectoprostatic angle on the right. Subtle capsular irregularity and possible early extracapsular spread is also noted at the bilateral mid-gland, most pronounced on the right posterior aspect. The overall extent of ECE is felt to be established rather than equivocal at the right apex.

SEMINAL VESICLES:
Bilateral seminal vesicle involvement is present. There is loss of the normal T2 hyperintensity within both seminal vesicles, replaced by hypointense tumor signal with associated restricted diffusion extending along the seminal vesicle-prostate junction. The involvement is more extensive on the right, where there is near-complete replacement of the right seminal vesicle. The left seminal vesicle shows involvement at its base with partial preservation of normal signal more distally. Findings are diagnostic of bilateral seminal vesicle invasion (T3b).

RECTUM:
The anterior rectal wall abuts the posterior prostate but there is no convincing evidence of direct rectal invasion. The mesorectal fascia appears intact.

BLADDER:
The bladder base and trigone are well-visualized. No direct extension of tumor into the bladder neck is seen. No bladder wall thickening or intraluminal masses.

PELVIC LYMPH NODES:
There is a lymph node in the right obturator fossa measuring 1.8 cm in short axis with a rounded contour, loss of the normal fatty hilum, and mildly heterogeneous internal signal on T2-weighted imaging. This node demonstrates mildly restricted diffusion. Morphologic features are highly suspicious for metastatic nodal involvement. Two additional right external iliac chain lymph nodes are identified, measuring 0.9 cm and 0.7 cm in short axis, respectively. Both demonstrate mild rounding and borderline loss of hilar architecture; the larger of these two (0.9 cm) is indeterminate to mildly suspicious for nodal involvement. A small left external iliac lymph node measuring 0.5 cm is present and appears morphologically benign. No left-sided obturator or internal iliac adenopathy.

BONY PELVIS:
The osseous structures of the pelvis are imaged on these sequences. No focal marrow signal abnormality suspicious for osseous metastatic disease is identified within the pelvis, sacrum, or imaged proximal femora. No cortical destruction.

OTHER PELVIC STRUCTURES:
No pelvic free fluid. No pelvic sidewall invasion. The neurovascular bundles are closely abutted bilaterally and are likely involved given the extent of posterolateral tumor; nerve-sparing surgery would not be appropriate given disease extent.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Bulky bilateral prostatic adenocarcinoma with a dominant lesion measuring approximately 3.4 cm, PI-RADS 5, occupying the peripheral zone bilaterally with dominant involvement of the right apex and bilateral mid-gland.

2. Definite extracapsular extension at the right apex; additional sites of capsular irregularity and possible ECE at the bilateral mid-gland posteriorly.

3. Bilateral seminal vesicle invasion, right greater than left, consistent with T3b disease.

4. Right obturator lymph node (1.8 cm) with morphologic features strongly suspicious for metastatic involvement. Two additional right-sided iliac chain lymph nodes (0.9 cm, 0.7 cm) are indeterminate; the 0.9 cm node is borderline suspicious.

5. No pelvic osseous lesions identified on MRI sequences.

6. Overall staging impression: at minimum cT3bN1, high-risk locally advanced prostate cancer. Correlation with systemic staging imaging (bone scan and cross-sectional CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis) is recommended to exclude distant metastatic disease.

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Mr. Hutchinson is a 72-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) who presents for oncology follow-up and treatment planning following discontinuation of olaparib due to disease progression with visceral metastases and cumulative treatment-related anemia. His case was reviewed at the GU Oncology Multidisciplinary Tumor Board earlier this week. He is accompanied today by his wife.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Hutchinson has a well-documented history of high-risk prostate adenocarcinoma (Gleason 9, cT3bN1M0) diagnosed approximately five years ago, initially treated with ADT plus abiraterone (Zytiga) and definitive IMRT to the prostate and pelvis. He enjoyed a prolonged biochemical response but developed castration-resistant disease approximately two years into that regimen with a PSA doubling time around 3.5 months. Germline testing at that time confirmed a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift mutation (c.5946delT), and Foundation One CDx on re-biopsy demonstrated biallelic BRCA2 loss, AR amplification, TP53 R175H, RB1 loss, and PTEN deletion. PSMA PET/CT at CRPC declaration showed low-volume disease: a right iliac chain node and a single early bone lesion at the right posterior iliac crest not seen on conventional bone scan.

He was transitioned to enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg daily given the AR amplification pattern on NGS and initial PSA response (61% decline). He achieved a PSA nadir of 0.7 ng/mL with stable imaging at 6 months. However, at 9 months he developed rapid biochemical and radiographic progression -- PSA doubling time of approximately 6 weeks -- with new bone metastases at T10, right 6th rib, and left iliac wing on bone scan and CT. MRI spine confirmed T10 vertebral body involvement without epidural extension or cord compromise. He received palliative RT to T10 (30 Gy in 10 fractions) concurrently with transitioning to olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg BID based on his biallelic BRCA2 loss.

On olaparib, he had an excellent initial PSA response (nadir 0.6 ng/mL at 6 months) with stable bone disease on restaging scans. However, restaging imaging at 6 months revealed a new 1.4 cm segment VII hepatic lesion, which was biopsied and confirmed as metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma without neuroendocrine transformation (PSMA-positive, NKX3.1-positive, Ki-67 35%). Olaparib was continued given bone and PSA stability, but over the subsequent 2 months PSA rose to 1.8 ng/mL (from nadir 0.6), meeting progression criteria.

Concurrently, Mr. Hutchinson has had significant difficulty with olaparib-related anemia. He developed grade 3 anemia (Hgb nadir 7.1 g/dL) at 3 months, requiring dose reduction to 200 mg BID and pRBC transfusion. He has since been transfusion-dependent with 8 units pRBC administered over the course of olaparib therapy (roughly every 4-6 weeks). Bone marrow biopsy obtained by hematology consultation showed erythroid hypoplasia consistent with PARP inhibitor toxicity; no marrow infiltration by tumor. He received IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) without meaningful improvement in transfusion independence. Given cumulative hematologic toxicity and now confirmed visceral + PSA progression, olaparib was discontinued at the last visit.

Repeat PSMA PET/CT obtained at olaparib discontinuation showed: 7 PSMA-avid bone lesions (T10, T12, L3, right 6th rib, left iliac wing, right posterior iliac crest, new right femoral head), PSMA-avid right iliac chain node (1.3 cm, SUVmax 6.1), and the segment VII hepatic lesion (SUVmax 8.4, now 1.8 cm). Notably, all lesions demonstrate strong PSMA avidity (SUVmax range 3.8-8.4). No brain lesions on skull survey.

His case was presented at GU Oncology Tumor Board. Multidisciplinary discussion included GU medical oncology, radiation oncology, nuclear medicine, palliative care, and pharmacy. The consensus recommendation, given his high tumor PSMA expression, post-ARSI/PARP-inhibitor status, and ECOG PS 2 with significant anemia, is to proceed with lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (177Lu-PSMA-617, Pluvicto) as next-line therapy. The team acknowledged that he has not yet received cabazitaxel-based chemotherapy; however, given his current performance status (ECOG 2), ongoing transfusion requirements, and strong patient preference to avoid IV cytotoxic chemotherapy, the group felt Pluvicto represented the most appropriate next step, noting that PSMA eligibility criteria are met. Palliative care remains actively co-managing symptoms. An extended goals-of-care conversation was held with Mr. Hutchinson and his wife.

He has been on continuous ADT plus abi for 36 months and has had an excellent initial response, but has now exhausted the activity of this regimen. I have explained to him and his wife that while PSA is rising and we need to reclassify the disease as "castration-resistant," he feels well today, and importantly, he is currently asymptomatic without bone pain or neurological symptoms. We discussed that CRPC does not mean untreatable -- there are multiple effective options and we will be pursuing a complete evaluation.

2. abi -- DISCONTINUATION
Given confirmed CRPC with PSA progression on Zytiga plus ADT, I am discontinuing abi and prednisone today. Continuing Lupron to maintain castrate testosterone level; there is strong evidence supporting ongoing ADT even after progression to CRPC. Patient understands.

3. GENETIC TESTING -- GERMLINE BRCA/HRR PANEL ORDERED
Given his family history of prostate cancer (father, paternal uncle), his very high-risk disease at diagnosis (Gleason 4+5=9, seminal vesicle invasion, lymph node involvement), and the clinical importance of identifying germline HRR alterations (particularly BRCA2) for therapeutic decision-making in CRPC (ola, ruca eligibility), I am referring him today for hereditary cancer genetic counseling and ordering a germline hereditary cancer panel (Myriad myRisk or equivalent). Results are expected within 2-3 weeks. Cascade testing of his sons will be recommended pending results.

4. SOMATIC TUMOR MOLECULAR PROFILING
Given CRPC, I am also arranging repeat tissue biopsy (likely prostate or lymph node re-biopsy) with submission for somatic next-generation sequencing (Foundation One CDx or equivalent) to evaluate for actionable alterations including AR amplification/mutations, BRCA2 biallelic inactivation, CDK12 mutation, MSI/TMB, and neuroendocrine markers. This will guide sequencing of second-line therapy. Biopsy to be scheduled separately.

5. RESTAGING IMAGING
Ordering restaging bone scan and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis. Additionally, I will be ordering a PSMA PET/CT (68Ga-PSMA-11) to evaluate for occult metastatic disease given the known limitations of conventional imaging in low-volume CRPC. PSMA PET may detect nodal or osseous metastases not seen on bone scan or CT, and will also help inform eligibility for future PSMA-directed therapies if needed.

6. NEXT-LINE THERAPY
Deferring initiation of new systemic therapy pending results of germline testing, somatic NGS, and restaging imaging. Potential options under consideration include:
   - Xtandi (Xtandi) or darolutamide -- next-generation AR-signaling inhibitors
   - Lynparza (Lynparza) -- PARP inhibitor, FDA-approved in mCRPC with HRR mutations; highly relevant if BRCA2 germline confirmed
   - doce -- if visceral disease or rapid progression identified
   - Clinical trial enrollment

7. BONE HEALTH
Patient has known osteopenia at baseline and is now entering CRPC. Will reassess bone-directed therapy (denosumab vs. zoledronic acid) once metastatic status is defined by imaging. Ongoing calcium and vitamin D supplementation.

8. DIABETES / HYPERTENSION
Both stable per recent labs. Continued on current regimens. Note: prednisone being discontinued -- monitor blood glucose closely over the next 2-4 weeks as glucose may change.

---

PLAN SUMMARY:
- Discontinue abi acetate and prednisone today
- Continue leup acetate q3 months (next injection due [next scheduled date])
- Order germline hereditary cancer panel (Myriad myRisk)
- Schedule prostate/lymph node re-biopsy for somatic NGS (Foundation One CDx)
- Order: restaging bone scan, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, PSMA PET/CT
- Follow-up appointment in 3-4 weeks to review imaging, biopsy, and genetic testing results and finalize second-line therapy plan
- Continue amlodipine and metformin; monitor blood glucose after prednisone discontinuation
- Patient and wife counseled extensively today regarding CRPC diagnosis, next steps, and treatment options. Patient expressing appropriate understanding and engaged in decision-making. Questions answered.

Time spent with patient and family: approximately 55 minutes.
